result.summary: Prevents attackers from using renamed or copied Windows system tools to evade detection.

steps: Click Endpoint security > Attack surface reduction
Set Platform to Windows 10, Windows 11, and Windows Server
Set Profile to Attack surface reduction rules
Enter name: ASR - Block Impersonated System Tools
Find Block abuse of exploited vulnerable signed drivers and set to Block
Click Next, assign to device groups, click Create
Create or edit a GPO linked to the target OU
Navigate to Computer Configuration > Administrative Templates > Windows Components > Microsoft Defender Antivirus > Microsoft Defender Exploit Guard > Attack Surface Reduction
Double-click Configure Attack Surface Reduction rules
Click Show and add: Value name = 56a863a9-875e-4185-98a7-b882c64b5ce5, Value = 1
gpo.policy_path: Navigate to Computer Configuration > Administrative Templates > Windows Components > Microsoft Defender Antivirus > Microsoft Defender Exploit Guard > Attack Surface Reduction
gpo.setting_name: Block use of copied or impersonated system tools

steps: Toggle Custom network indicators to On
Go to Rules > Attack surface reduction rules
Enable rule: Block abuse of exploited vulnerable signed drivers

validation_steps: In Intune portal (intune.microsoft.com) > Devices > Configuration — confirm the policy shows 'Succeeded' on target devices
Check device compliance: Intune > Reports > Device compliance > select the policy and verify assignment
On a target machine run: gpresult /r — confirm the GPO appears under 'Applied Group Policy Objects'
Run rsop.msc on a target device to verify the specific setting is applied with the correct value

rollback.intune: Remove the configuration policy or set to Not Configured in Intune
rollback.gpo: Set the policy to Not Configured in Group Policy Management
rollback.defender: Revert the policy in the Microsoft 365 Defender portal

risks: High severity — test on a pilot group before broad deployment to avoid disrupting business operations
ASR rules in Block mode can impact legitimate applications — consider enabling in Audit mode first to identify false positives before switching to Block
Apply to a test OU first using GPO. Run gpresult /r to confirm the policy applies correctly before linking to production OUs

references.title: Block use of copied or impersonated system tools

scripts.detection:
# Exit 0 = compliant, Exit 1 = not compliant
$ruleId = '56a863a9-875e-4185-98a7-b882c64b5ce5'
$pref = Get-MpPreference
$ids = @($pref.AttackSurfaceReductionRules_Ids)
$actions = @($pref.AttackSurfaceReductionRules_Actions)
$idx = $ids.IndexOf($ruleId)
if ($idx -ge 0 -and $actions[$idx] -eq 1) { exit 0 } else { exit 1 }
scripts.validation:
# Re-run detection script to confirm compliance
# Exit 0 = compliant, Exit 1 = not compliant

rollback.intune: Remove or disable the policy in Intune > Endpoint security or Devices > Configuration
rollback.gpo: Set the policy to 'Not Configured' in Group Policy Management Console
rollback.powershell:
# Rollback: Remove ASR rule
Remove-MpPreference -AttackSurfaceReductionRules_Ids '56a863a9-875e-4185-98a7-b882c64b5ce5'
Write-Output "ASR rule removed"

result.summary: Dual-privileged accounts in both Entra ID and on-prem AD are a higher risk if compromised.

gpo.policy_path: Computer Configuration > Windows Settings > Security Settings
gpo.setting_name: Identify Entra ID privileged accounts that are also privileged in Active Directory

steps: Click Identity > Roles & admins > Roles & admins
Review all Global Administrator and other privileged role assignments
Cross-reference with on-prem AD groups: Domain Admins, Enterprise Admins
For accounts found in both: go to Users > All users and open the account
Create new cloud-only admin accounts: Users > New user (ensure no on-prem sync)
Assign only required roles using PIM if available
Remove admin roles from synced accounts
Click Identity in the left navigation
Click Security posture > Identity secure score
Find Identify Entra ID privileged accounts that are also privileged in Active Directory
Review all accounts in the Exposed entities tab
For each account determine if dual-privilege is required
Action: create separate cloud-only accounts for Entra ID admin roles
Remove on-prem AD privileged group membership from accounts serving cloud-only admin functions

validation_steps: In Entra admin center (entra.microsoft.com) > Identity > Monitoring & health > Sign-in logs — confirm policy is enforced
In Microsoft 365 Defender (security.microsoft.com) > Secure Score — verify the recommendation 'Identify Entra ID privileged accounts that are als' status changes to completed
Wait 24 hours and check Microsoft Secure Score — the recommendation should move to 'Completed' or show increased score points

rollback.intune: Remove the configuration policy or set to Not Configured in Intune
rollback.gpo: Set the policy to Not Configured in Group Policy Management
rollback.entra: Revert the setting in the Entra admin center
rollback.defender: Revert the policy in the Microsoft 365 Defender portal

risks: High severity — test on a pilot group before broad deployment to avoid disrupting business operations

references.title: Identify Entra ID privileged accounts that are also privileged in Active Directory

scripts.detection:
# Verify manually in admin portal
Write-Output 'Check in admin portal'
exit 0
scripts.implementation:
Write-Output '1. Go to entra.microsoft.com > Identity > Roles & admins'
Write-Output '2. Review all privileged role assignments'
Write-Output '3. Remove on-prem privileged accounts from cloud admin roles'
scripts.validation: # Re-run detection script to confirm compliance

rollback.intune: Remove policy in Intune portal
rollback.gpo: Set to Not Configured in GPMC
rollback.entra: Re-assign roles in Entra ID admin center if needed
rollback.powershell: Write-Output 'Re-assign roles via entra.microsoft.com > Identity > Roles & admins'

result.summary: LDAP signing ensures directory communications are not tampered with, preventing man-in-the-middle attacks.

steps: Set Platform to Windows 10 and later
Set Profile type to Settings catalog
Enter name: LDAP Server Signing Required
Search for LDAP server signing requirements
Enable Domain controller LDAP server signing requirements
Click Next, assign to Domain Controllers device group, click Create
Create or edit a GPO linked to Domain Controllers OU
Navigate to Computer Configuration > Windows Settings > Security Settings > Local Policies > Security Options
Find Domain controller: LDAP server signing requirements
Find Network security: LDAP client signing requirements
Run gpupdate /force on all Domain Controllers
gpo.policy_path: Navigate to Computer Configuration > Windows Settings > Security Settings > Local Policies > Security Options
gpo.setting_name: Require LDAP server signing to ensure integrity of directory traffic

validation_steps: In Intune portal (intune.microsoft.com) > Devices > Configuration — confirm the policy shows 'Succeeded' on target devices

Run rsop.msc on a target device to verify the specific setting is applied with the correct value

rollback.gpo: Set the policy to Not Configured in Group Policy Management

references.title: Require LDAP server signing to ensure integrity of directory traffic

scripts.validation:
# Re-run detection script to confirm compliance
# Exit 0 = compliant (signing required), Exit 1 = not compliant

rollback.intune: Remove or disable the policy in Intune > Endpoint security or Devices > Configuration
rollback.gpo: Set the policy to 'Not Configured' in Group Policy Management Console
rollback.powershell:
# Rollback: Set LDAP signing to negotiated (1)
Set-ItemProperty 'HKLM:\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\NTDS\Parameters' -Name 'LDAPServerIntegrity' -Value 1 -Type DWord
Write-Output "LDAP server signing reverted to Negotiate signing"

result.summary: NTLM is vulnerable to pass-the-hash and relay attacks. Disabling it forces use of Kerberos.

steps: Set Platform to Windows 10 and later, Profile type to Settings catalog
Find Network Security Restrict NTLM Outgoing NTLM Traffic To Remote Servers
Find Network Security Restrict NTLM NTLM Authentication In This Domain
TIP: First set to Audit all in a test ring before enabling full block
Click Next, assign to device groups, click Create
Create or edit a GPO linked to the domain or target OU
Navigate to Computer Configuration > Windows Settings > Security Settings > Local Policies > Security Options
Find Network security: Restrict NTLM: NTLM authentication in this domain
Set to Deny all (use Audit all first to assess impact)
Find Network security: Restrict NTLM: Outgoing NTLM traffic to remote servers
Find Network security: LAN Manager authentication level
Set to Send NTLMv2 response only. Refuse LM & NTLM
Run gpupdate /force and monitor Event IDs 4624 and 4625 before full enforcement
gpo.policy_path: Navigate to Computer Configuration > Windows Settings > Security Settings > Local Policies > Security Options
gpo.setting_name: Disable NTLM authentication for Windows

validation_steps: In Intune portal (intune.microsoft.com) > Devices > Configuration — confirm the policy shows 'Succeeded' on target devices
Check device compliance: Intune > Reports > Device compliance > select the policy and verify assignment
On a target machine run: gpresult /r — confirm the GPO appears under 'Applied Group Policy Objects'
Run rsop.msc on a target device to verify the specific setting is applied with the correct value

rollback.intune: Remove the configuration policy or set to Not Configured in Intune
rollback.gpo: Set the policy to Not Configured in Group Policy Management

risks: High severity — test on a pilot group before broad deployment to avoid disrupting business operations
Changes to authentication settings can lock out users — communicate the change in advance and have a rollback plan ready
Changes to authentication protocols can break legacy applications that rely on older protocols — inventory and test all applications before deployment

scripts.validation:
# Re-run detection script to confirm compliance
# Exit 0 = compliant (NTLM restricted), Exit 1 = not compliant

rollback.intune: Remove or disable the policy in Intune > Endpoint security or Devices > Configuration
rollback.gpo: Set the policy to 'Not Configured' in Group Policy Management Console
rollback.powershell:
# Rollback: Re-enable NTLM
$path = 'HKLM:\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Lsa\MSV1_0'
Set-ItemProperty $path -Name 'RestrictReceivingNTLMTraffic' -Value 0 -Type DWord
Set-ItemProperty $path -Name 'RestrictSendingNTLMTraffic' -Value 0 -Type DWord
Write-Output "NTLM restrictions removed"

references.title: Set 'Minimum PIN length for startup' to '6 or more characters'

scripts.detection: $v=(Get-ItemProperty 'HKLM:\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\FVE' -Name 'MinimumPIN' -EA 0).MinimumPIN; if ($v -ge 6) { exit 0 } else { exit 1 }
scripts.implementation:
$p='HKLM:\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\FVE'
if (-not (Test-Path $p)) { New-Item $p -Force | Out-Null }
Set-ItemProperty $p -Name MinimumPIN -Value 6 -Type DWord
Write-Output "BitLocker minimum PIN set to 6"
scripts.validation: # Re-run detection script to confirm compliance

rollback.gpo: Set to Not Configured in GPMC

result.summary: Configuring sign-in frequency and disabling persistent sessions for admins reduces session hijacking risk.

gpo.policy_path: Computer Configuration > Windows Settings > Security Settings
gpo.setting_name: Ensure Sign-in frequency is enabled and browser sessions are not persistent for Administrative users

steps: Name the policy: Admin - Sign-in Frequency and No Persistent Session
Under Users click Select users and groups > Directory roles
Select all admin roles: Global Admin, User Admin, Security Admin, Exchange Admin, SharePoint Admin, Compliance Admin
Under Target resources select All cloud apps
Set to Enabled and enter 1 Hours
Under Session click Persistent browser session
Under Grant ensure Require multifactor authentication is selected

exit 0

Write-Output '2. Create/edit policy targeting admin roles'
Write-Output '3. Session > Sign-in frequency: 1 hour + Never persistent browser session'
Write-Output '4. Enable policy'

steps: Cannot be done via GPO. Use PowerShell on a Domain Controller:

result.summary: Encrypting LDAP traffic via LDAPS on port 636 prevents eavesdropping on directory data.

steps: Set Platform to Windows 10 and later, Profile type to Settings catalog
Click Create, name: LDAP Traffic Encryption
Find Domain controller LDAP server signing requirements
Click Next, assign to Domain Controllers group, click Create
First enable LDAPS on Domain Controllers by installing a valid certificate:
The certificate must have Server Authentication EKU and the DC FQDN as the Subject
Issue from internal PKI and install on all DCs
Navigate to Computer Configuration > Windows Settings > Security Settings > Local Policies > Security Options
Set Domain controller: LDAP server signing requirements to Require signing
Set Network security: LDAP client signing requirements to Require signing
Test LDAPS: open ldp.exe > Connection > Connect on port 636 with SSL checked

scripts.detection:
# Exit 0 = compliant (clipboard/drive redirect disabled), Exit 1 = not compliant
$val = (Get-ItemProperty 'HKLM:\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\Windows NT\Terminal Services' -Name 'fDisableClip' -EA SilentlyContinue).fDisableClip
$drives = (Get-ItemProperty 'HKLM:\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\Windows NT\Terminal Services' -Name 'fDisableCdm' -EA SilentlyContinue).fDisableCdm
if ($val -eq 1 -and $drives -eq 1) { exit 0 } else { exit 1 }
scripts.validation:
# Re-run detection script to confirm compliance
# Exit 0 = compliant (clipboard/drive redirect disabled), Exit 1 = not compliant

rollback.intune: Remove or disable the policy in Intune > Endpoint security or Devices > Configuration
rollback.gpo: Set the policy to 'Not Configured' in Group Policy Management Console
rollback.powershell:
# Rollback: Re-enable RDP clipboard and drive redirection
$path = 'HKLM:\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\Windows NT\Terminal Services'
Set-ItemProperty $path -Name 'fDisableClip' -Value 0 -Type DWord
Set-ItemProperty $path -Name 'fDisableCdm' -Value 0 -Type DWord

result.summary: Unquoted service paths with spaces allow DLL hijacking via Windows path traversal.

steps: Find unquoted paths with PowerShell: Get-WmiObject Win32_Service | Where-Object {$_.PathName -match ' ' -and $_.PathName -notmatch '"'} | Select Name, PathName
For each service found, wrap the path in quotes:
Edit the ImagePath value to wrap the path in quotes: "C:\Program Files\Vendor\service.exe"
Or use: sc config <ServiceName> binpath= "\"C:\Path With Space\service.exe\""
Restart the service to verify it still works
gpo.policy_path: Computer Configuration > Windows Settings > Security Settings
gpo.setting_name: Fix unquoted service path for Windows services

steps: Find Fix unquoted service path for Windows services
Review all exposed devices and services listed

Run rsop.msc on a target device to verify the specific setting is applied with the correct value
In Microsoft 365 Defender (security.microsoft.com) > Secure Score — verify the recommendation 'Fix unquoted service path for Windows services' status changes to completed
Wait 24 hours and check Microsoft Secure Score — the recommendation should move to 'Completed' or show increased score points

rollback.gpo: Set the policy to Not Configured in Group Policy Management
rollback.defender: Revert the policy in the Microsoft 365 Defender portal

result.summary: Services running from user-writable locations can be hijacked. Move them to protected system directories.

steps: Identify services in non-standard paths: Get-WmiObject Win32_Service | Where-Object {$_.PathName -notmatch 'C:\\Windows\\' -and $_.PathName -notmatch 'C:\\Program Files'} | Select Name, PathName
For each service in a writable location:
Move the executable to C:\Program Files\<VendorName>\
Update the service path: sc config <ServiceName> binpath= "\"C:\Program Files\Vendor\service.exe\""
Ensure the new folder has correct ACLs (non-admin users should not have write access)

result.summary: Secure Boot ensures only trusted bootloaders run during startup, protecting against bootkits.

steps: Set Platform to Windows 10 and later
Click Create, name: Require UEFI Secure Boot
Under System Security find Require Secure Boot to be enabled on the device
Assign to device groups, click Create
NOTE: Secure Boot must be enabled in UEFI firmware on each device — this policy validates the compliance state
Secure Boot is a UEFI firmware setting and cannot be enabled via GPO
1. Restart the device and enter UEFI/BIOS (usually F2, F10, or Del during POST)
2. Navigate to the Boot or Security section
4. Set Boot mode to UEFI (not Legacy or CSM)
For enterprise scale use SCCM/MECM or vendor BIOS management tools
gpo.policy_path: Computer Configuration > Windows Settings > Security Settings
gpo.setting_name: Enable UEFI Secure Boot mode

result.summary: Service accounts with cached passwords in the registry are vulnerable to credential harvesting.

steps: Replace regular service accounts with Group Managed Service Accounts (gMSA):
Update the service to use the gMSA: sc config <ServiceName> obj= domain\gMSA_ServiceName$
This eliminates the stored password entirely
Verify the service starts correctly with the gMSA account
gpo.policy_path: Computer Configuration > Windows Settings > Security Settings
gpo.setting_name: Change service account to avoid cached password in windows registry

steps: Find Change service account to avoid cached password in windows registry

validation_steps: On a target machine run: gpresult /r — confirm the GPO appears under 'Applied Group Policy Objects'
Run rsop.msc on a target device to verify the specific setting is applied with the correct value
In Microsoft 365 Defender (security.microsoft.com) > Secure Score — verify the recommendation 'Change service account to avoid cached password in' status changes to completed
Wait 24 hours and check Microsoft Secure Score — the recommendation should move to 'Completed' or show increased score points

rollback.intune: Remove the configuration policy or set to Not Configured in Intune
rollback.gpo: Set the policy to Not Configured in Group Policy Management
rollback.defender: Revert the policy in the Microsoft 365 Defender portal

risks: High severity — test on a pilot group before broad deployment to avoid disrupting business operations

references.title: Change service account to avoid cached password in windows registry

scripts.detection:
$svcs = Get-WmiObject Win32_Service | Where-Object { $_.StartName -ne 'LocalSystem' -and $_.StartName -ne 'LocalService' -and $_.StartName -ne 'NetworkService' -and $_.StartName }
if ($svcs.Count -gt 0) { Write-Output "Services with custom accounts: $($svcs.Count)"; exit 1 } else { exit 0 }
scripts.implementation:
Write-Output 'Convert service accounts to gMSA:'
Write-Output "New-ADServiceAccount -Name 'svc_gMSA' -ManagedPasswordIntervalInDays 30"
Write-Output "sc config ServiceName obj='DOMAIN\svc_gMSA$' password=''"
scripts.validation: # Re-run detection script to confirm compliance

rollback.intune: Remove policy in Intune portal
rollback.gpo: Set to Not Configured in GPMC

scripts.detection:
# Exit 0 = compliant, Exit 1 = not compliant
$policy = net accounts | Select-String 'Lockout threshold'
$threshold = ($policy -split ':')[1].Trim()
if ($threshold -ne 'Never' -and [int]$threshold -ge 1 -and [int]$threshold -le 10) { exit 0 } else { exit 1 }

scripts.detection: $v=(Get-ItemProperty 'HKLM:\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\System' -Name EnableSmartScreen -EA 0).EnableSmartScreen; if ($v -ge 1) { exit 0 } else { exit 1 }
scripts.implementation:
$p='HKLM:\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\System'
if (-not (Test-Path $p)) { New-Item $p -Force | Out-Null }
Set-ItemProperty $p -Name EnableSmartScreen -Value 1 -Type DWord
Set-ItemProperty $p -Name ShellSmartScreenLevel -Value 'Block' -Type String
Write-Output "SmartScreen enabled for apps and files"

scripts.detection: $v=(Get-ItemProperty 'HKLM:\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\LanmanWorkstation' -Name AllowInsecureGuestAuth -EA 0).AllowInsecureGuestAuth; if ($v -eq 0 -or $null -eq $v) { exit 0 } else { exit 1 }
scripts.implementation:
$p='HKLM:\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\LanmanWorkstation'
if (-not (Test-Path $p)) { New-Item $p -Force | Out-Null }
Set-ItemProperty $p -Name AllowInsecureGuestAuth -Value 0 -Type DWord
Write-Output "Insecure SMB guest logons blocked"
scripts.validation: # Re-run detection script to confirm compliance

rollback.gpo: Set to Not Configured in GPMC

result.summary: Removing write access for Everyone from network shares prevents unauthorized file modification.

steps: Open Server Manager on each file server
Go to File and Storage Services > Shares
For each share: right-click > Properties > Permissions > Customize permissions
In Security tab: remove Everyone from having Write or Full Control access
In Share Permissions tab: remove Everyone if present, replace with Authenticated Users with Read
Alternatively via PowerShell on the file server:
Grant correct access: Grant-SmbShareAccess -Name <ShareName> -AccountName 'Authenticated Users' -AccessRight Read

Note: C$, ADMIN$, IPC$ are default administrative shares — see Microsoft guidance before removing these

scripts.detection: $r=Get-SmbShare | Where-Object { $_.Path -match '^[A-Za-z]:\\$' }; if ($r.Count -eq 0) { exit 0 } else { Write-Output "Root shares: $($r.Name)"; exit 1 }
scripts.implementation: Get-SmbShare | Where-Object { $_.Path -match '^[A-Za-z]:\\$' } | ForEach-Object { Remove-SmbShare -Name $_.Name -Force -Confirm:$false; Write-Output "Removed: $($_.Name)" }

result.summary: Dormant accounts in privileged groups cannot be monitored. Attackers can use them undetected.

steps: Find dormant accounts with PowerShell: Search-ADAccount -AccountInactive -TimeSpan 90.00:00:00 -UsersOnly
Step 1 - Disable accounts first: Search-ADAccount -AccountInactive -TimeSpan 90.00:00:00 -UsersOnly | Disable-ADAccount
Step 2 - Move to a Disabled Accounts OU for a quarantine period
Step 3 - After 30 to 60 days with no issues: Get-ADUser -SearchBase 'OU=Disabled,DC=domain,DC=com' -Filter * | Remove-ADUser

@('TLS_RSA_WITH_RC4_128_SHA','TLS_RSA_WITH_RC4_128_MD5','SSL_CK_RC4_128_WITH_MD5') | ForEach-Object { Disable-TlsCipherSuite -Name $_ -EA SilentlyContinue; Write-Output "Disabled: $_" }

result.summary: Custom WSL kernels bypass the MDE WSL monitoring plug-in, creating a security blind spot.

steps: Platform: Windows 10 and later, Profile type: Settings catalog
Search for WSL settings related to kernel customization
Set to block custom kernel or commandline arguments
Assign to device groups, click Create
Navigate to the WSL configuration file: %USERPROFILE%\.wslconfig or C:\Users\<username>\.wslconfig
Remove or comment out any custom kernel= or commandLine= entries under [wsl2]
Verify: wsl --status to confirm the default kernel is in use
For organizational enforcement configure via Intune (see above)

references.title: Turn off custom kernel/commandline in Windows Subsystem for Linux

scripts.detection: $v=(Get-ItemProperty 'HKLM:\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\Lxss' -Name AllowCustomKernelConfiguration -EA 0).AllowCustomKernelConfiguration; if ($v -eq 0) { exit 0 } else { exit 1 }
scripts.implementation:
$p='HKLM:\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\Lxss'
if (-not (Test-Path $p)) { New-Item $p -Force | Out-Null }
Set-ItemProperty $p -Name AllowCustomKernelConfiguration -Value 0 -Type DWord
Write-Output "WSL custom kernel disabled"
scripts.validation: # Re-run detection script to confirm compliance

rollback.intune: Remove policy in Intune portal
rollback.gpo: Set to Not Configured in GPMC

Go to Identity > Roles & admins to assign required admin roles using PIM where available
For existing synced admin accounts: check the user's On-premises sync enabled property
If synced: assign roles to the new cloud-only accounts and remove from synced accounts

The account should never be used legitimately in your environment

result.summary: Automatically blocking users who exceed message limits stops ongoing spam from compromised accounts.

gpo.policy_path: Computer Configuration > Windows Settings > Security Settings
gpo.setting_name: Block users who reached the message limit

steps: Click Email & collaboration > Policies & rules > Threat policies
Under Action when a user exceeds the message limits
Set to Restrict the user from sending email until the following day

Wait 24 hours and check Microsoft Secure Score — the recommendation should move to 'Completed' or show increased score points

result.summary: SSPR reduces helpdesk load and allows users to securely reset their own passwords without admin intervention.

gpo.policy_path: Computer Configuration > Windows Settings > Security Settings
gpo.setting_name: Ensure 'Self service password reset enabled' is set to 'All'

steps: Set Self service password reset enabled to All
Set Number of methods required to reset to 2
Enable: Microsoft Authenticator app, Email, Mobile phone
Set Require users to register when signing in to Yes

validation_steps: In Entra admin center (entra.microsoft.com) > Identity > Monitoring & health > Sign-in logs — confirm policy is enforced
Wait 24 hours and check Microsoft Secure Score — the recommendation should move to 'Completed' or show increased score points

result.summary: At least two Global Admins ensures business continuity if one account is compromised or becomes unavailable.

gpo.policy_path: Computer Configuration > Windows Settings > Security Settings
gpo.setting_name: Designate more than one global admin

steps: Click Identity > Roles & admins > Roles & admins
Review the current list of members
If fewer than 2 members click + Add assignments
Search for and select a second dedicated admin account
Best practice: use dedicated cloud-only accounts for all Global Admins
Also configure emergency break-glass accounts with Global Admin and store credentials securely offline

validation_steps: In Entra admin center (entra.microsoft.com) > Identity > Monitoring & health > Sign-in logs — confirm policy is enforced
Wait 24 hours and check Microsoft Secure Score — the recommendation should move to 'Completed' or show increased score points

result.summary: Assigning minimum required permissions reduces the blast radius if an admin account is compromised.

gpo.policy_path: Computer Configuration > Windows Settings > Security Settings
gpo.setting_name: Use least privileged administrative roles

steps: Click Identity > Roles & admins > Roles & admins
For each Global Admin assess whether a lesser role would suffice:
User management tasks: User Administrator role
Click + Add assignments on the appropriate lesser role and assign the account
Remove the Global Admin role: click on the account > click Remove
Enable PIM: click Identity Governance > Privileged Identity Management
Convert permanent role assignments to Eligible (require activation with MFA and justification)

validation_steps: In Entra admin center (entra.microsoft.com) > Identity > Monitoring & health > Sign-in logs — confirm policy is enforced
Wait 24 hours and check Microsoft Secure Score — the recommendation should move to 'Completed' or show increased score points
